#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# tools/qemu-run.sh -- cargo runner for the aarch64 kernel target.
#
# Cargo invokes this with the kernel ELF path as $1. We build a tiny ESP
# directory tree on the fly and hand it to QEMU's `-drive file=fat:rw:DIR`
# helper, which exposes the directory as a FAT32 filesystem. That avoids
# needing parted/mtools/hdiutil to land a "MINIX 4 booting" banner.
#
# Override the UEFI firmware location with QEMU_EFI_AARCH64=/path/to/fw.fd.

set -euo pipefail

if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "usage: $0 <kernel-elf> [extra qemu args...]" >&2
    exit 64
fi

KERNEL="$1"
shift

ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
ESP="$ROOT/target/esp"
LIMINE_BIN="$ROOT/external/limine/dist/BOOTAA64.EFI"

# Materialize the Limine binary if `make` hasn't been run yet.
if [[ ! -f "$LIMINE_BIN" ]]; then
    echo "==> Fetching Limine binaries (one-time)..." >&2
    make -C "$ROOT/external/limine" >&2
fi

# Locate aarch64 UEFI firmware. Honor explicit override first.
FIRMWARE_CANDIDATES=(
    "${QEMU_EFI_AARCH64:-}"
    "$(brew --prefix qemu 2>/dev/null || true)/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/opt/homebrew/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/usr/local/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/usr/share/edk2-aarch64/QEMU_EFI.fd"
    "/usr/share/AAVMF/AAVMF_CODE.fd"
)
FIRMWARE=""
for cand in "${FIRMWARE_CANDIDATES[@]}"; do
    if [[ -n "$cand" && -f "$cand" ]]; then
        FIRMWARE="$cand"
        break
    fi
done
if [[ -z "$FIRMWARE" ]]; then
    cat >&2 <<'EOF'
error: no aarch64 UEFI firmware found.
       Install QEMU (brew install qemu) or point QEMU_EFI_AARCH64 at an
       OVMF/edk2 firmware blob, e.g.:
           export QEMU_EFI_AARCH64=/path/to/QEMU_EFI.fd
EOF
    exit 69
fi

# Stage the ESP directory tree.
rm -rf "$ESP"
mkdir -p "$ESP/EFI/BOOT" "$ESP/boot"
cp "$LIMINE_BIN" "$ESP/EFI/BOOT/BOOTAA64.EFI"
cp "$ROOT/tools/limine.conf" "$ESP/limine.conf"
cp "$KERNEL" "$ESP/boot/kernel"

exec qemu-system-aarch64 \
    -M virt -cpu cortex-a72 -m 256M \
    -bios "$FIRMWARE" \
    -drive "file=fat:rw:fat-type=32:$ESP,format=raw,if=virtio" \
    -display none \
    -serial stdio \
    -no-reboot \
    "$@"

1	/*
2	 * MINIX 4 aarch64 kernel linker script
3	 *
4	 * Higher-half kernel placed at 0xffffffff80000000 -- matches docs/memory-layout.md
5	 * and Limine's default aarch64 kernel base. The Limine bootloader scans the ELF
6	 * for a `.limine_requests` section bracketed by start/end markers, fills in
7	 * response pointers, sets up translation tables (the kernel is mapped at the
8	 * link address), and jumps to `_start`.
9	 */
10	
11	OUTPUT_FORMAT(elf64-littleaarch64)
12	OUTPUT_ARCH(aarch64)
13	ENTRY(_start)
14	
15	PHDRS {
16	    requests PT_LOAD FLAGS(4); /* R   */
17	    text     PT_LOAD FLAGS(5); /* R+X */
18	    rodata   PT_LOAD FLAGS(4); /* R   */
19	    data     PT_LOAD FLAGS(6); /* RW  */
20	}
21	
22	SECTIONS {
23	    . = 0xffffffff80000000;
24	
25	    .limine_requests : {
26	        KEEP(*(.limine_requests_start))
27	        KEEP(*(.limine_requests))
28	        KEEP(*(.limine_requests_end))
29	    } :requests
30	
31	    . = ALIGN(4K);
32	    .text : {
33	        KEEP(*(.text.boot))
34	        *(.text .text.*)
35	    } :text
36	
37	    . = ALIGN(4K);
38	    .rodata : {
39	        *(.rodata .rodata.*)
40	    } :rodata
41	
42	    . = ALIGN(4K);
43	    .data : {
44	        *(.data .data.*)
45	    } :data
46	
47	    .bss : {
48	        __bss_start = .;
49	        *(.bss .bss.*)
50	        *(COMMON)
51	        __bss_end = .;
52	    } :data
53	
54	    /DISCARD/ : {
55	        *(.eh_frame)
56	        *(.eh_frame_hdr)
57	        *(.note .note.*)
58	        *(.comment)
59	    }
60	}
61

content.text: I have enough context now. Let me confirm two design decisions with you before writing the plan.

questions.question: How should the two EL0 stub tasks 'print interleaved bursts' to prove preemption?

options.label: Kernel prints per-tick (Recommended)
options.description: EL0 stubs busy-loop on a counter. Kernel timer ISR prints one char per tick identifying the preempted task (e.g. "AABBBAAAABBB"). Keeps EL0 isolated from device MMIO; matches the microkernel model where drivers (not user tasks) own the UART.

options.label: EL0 stubs poke PL011 directly
options.description: Map the PL011 MMIO page into each task's TTBR0 as EL0-writable. Each stub writes its identifying char in a tight loop. Visually striking — interruptions land mid-burst — but it grants EL0 unfettered MMIO access that we'd rip out in Phase 5 anyway.

options.label: EL0 stubs use SVC to log
options.description: Each stub does an `svc #0` with a special debug call number; the kernel handles it and writes the char. Closer to slice 2.5 IPC but doesn't actually exercise IPC — it's a fake path we'd delete.

questions.question: TTBR0 layout for two EL0 stub tasks?

options.label: Shared TTBR0, different VAs (Recommended)
options.description: Extend the existing single TTBR0 walk: add a third L3 (or reuse) so task A maps to one code+stack pair and task B to another at adjacent VAs. No TTBR0_EL1 swap on context switch. Simplest; matches the plan's statement that proper per-process AS is Phase 3.

options.label: Per-task TTBR0, swap on switch
options.description: Build two independent L0 trees (one per task) and write TTBR0_EL1 on each context switch. Closer to the real per-process model and introduces TLB-flush plumbing early, but extra ~40 LoC of MMU code and TLBI sequencing for a property Phase 3 will rebuild anyway.

You can now continue with these answers in mind.

questions.question: How should the two EL0 stub tasks 'print interleaved bursts' to prove preemption?

options.label: Kernel prints per-tick (Recommended)
options.description: EL0 stubs busy-loop on a counter. Kernel timer ISR prints one char per tick identifying the preempted task (e.g. "AABBBAAAABBB"). Keeps EL0 isolated from device MMIO; matches the microkernel model where drivers (not user tasks) own the UART.

options.label: EL0 stubs poke PL011 directly
options.description: Map the PL011 MMIO page into each task's TTBR0 as EL0-writable. Each stub writes its identifying char in a tight loop. Visually striking — interruptions land mid-burst — but it grants EL0 unfettered MMIO access that we'd rip out in Phase 5 anyway.

options.label: EL0 stubs use SVC to log
options.description: Each stub does an `svc #0` with a special debug call number; the kernel handles it and writes the char. Closer to slice 2.5 IPC but doesn't actually exercise IPC — it's a fake path we'd delete.

questions.question: TTBR0 layout for two EL0 stub tasks?

options.label: Shared TTBR0, different VAs (Recommended)
options.description: Extend the existing single TTBR0 walk: add a third L3 (or reuse) so task A maps to one code+stack pair and task B to another at adjacent VAs. No TTBR0_EL1 swap on context switch. Simplest; matches the plan's statement that proper per-process AS is Phase 3.

options.label: Per-task TTBR0, swap on switch
options.description: Build two independent L0 trees (one per task) and write TTBR0_EL1 on each context switch. Closer to the real per-process model and introduces TLB-flush plumbing early, but extra ~40 LoC of MMU code and TLBI sequencing for a property Phase 3 will rebuild anyway.

content.text: Now I'll write the plan. Both recommended choices selected — kernel-side per-tick logging and shared TTBR0 with separate VAs.

input.content:
# Slice 2.4 — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues

## Context

Slice 2.3 shipped a single-task cooperative EL0 path: kmain → `userland_bootstrap` → `switch_to_user` erets into one EL0 stub that executes `svc #0` in a loop; each SVC lands in `do_ipc()`, logs to UART, returns to EL0. Phase 2's heart is preemptive scheduling, and slice 2.4 is the slice where time actually flows: the ARM generic timer raises PPI 27 through GICv3, the kernel ISR decrements the current task's quantum, and when the quantum hits zero the scheduler picks a different runnable task. Two EL0 stub tasks at the same priority will round-robin under preemption, and the kernel ISR prints one character per tick identifying the running task — output like `AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBB...` proves both tasks got CPU time and the timer drove the switches.

This satisfies the slice 2.4 milestone in `docs/plan.md:446`: *"Two EL0 stub tasks print interleaved bursts proving timer-driven preemption."* Slice 2.5 will replace the `svc #0` stub body with real IPC primitives; slice 2.6 will dispatch real kernel calls.

## Settled design decisions

- **Kernel-side per-tick logging.** EL0 stubs busy-loop (no MMIO from EL0). The kernel ISR writes one char from `current_proc.name[0]` to PL011 on each tick. Output groups in quantum-sized bursts because preemption only happens at quantum-exhaust.
- **Shared TTBR0, two VAs.** Extend the existing TTBR0 walk: task A keeps `USER_CODE_VA = 0x40_0000` / `USER_STACK_VA = 0x80_0000`, task B gets adjacent VAs (`USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`). No TTBR0_EL1 swap on context switch — Phase 3's VM server builds per-process address spaces.
- **No special idle handling.** If `pick_proc` returns `None`, the kernel halts in WFE with IRQs unmasked. Boot-image kernel-task slots (ASYNCM, IDLE, CLOCK, SYSTEM, HARDWARE) and server slots are *not* enqueued — they have no real EL0 context yet. Only the two stub slots enter the run queue.
- **Kernel runs DAIF-masked.** IRQs are unmasked only while EL0 runs (via SPSR bit clear). Slot 5 (el1h_irq) stays panic-on-trap for slice 2.4.

## Files to create

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/gic.rs` — GICv3 driver: HHDM-relative base resolution (`GICD = 0x0800_0000`, `GICR = 0x080A_0000` on QEMU virt cortex-a72), `init()` (distributor + redistributor wake + ICC_SRE/PMR/IGRPEN), `enable_ppi(intid, priority)`, `ack() -> u32` (ICC_IAR1_EL1), `eoi(intid)` (ICC_EOIR1_EL1).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/timer.rs` — ARM generic timer (virtual): `init(tick_hz)` reads `CNTFRQ_EL0`, programs `CNTV_TVAL_EL0`, sets `CNTV_CTL_EL0 = 1`; `rearm()` reloads TVAL each ISR; `INTID_VIRT_TIMER = 27` constant.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` — `el0_64_irq_entry`: save EL0 GPRs and SP_EL0/ELR/SPSR into `tpidr_el1`-pointed frame (mirrors `trap.S` idiom for `el0_64_sync_entry`), `bl do_irq`, tail-call `el1_return_to_user`. Added to `kernel/build.rs` sources.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/irq.rs` — `pub extern "C" fn do_irq(frame: &mut ArchRegisterFrame)`: ack via `gic::ack()`, dispatch by intid (PPI 27 → `clock::tick()`, else panic), eoi via `gic::eoi(intid)`. Returns; `el1_return_to_user` reads (possibly-new) `tpidr_el1`.
- `kernel/src/clock.rs` — arch-independent tick handler: increment `UPTIME` (`AtomicU64`); decrement `current_proc.quantum_left`; when it reaches 0, set `RTS_NO_QUANTUM` and call `sched::reschedule()`; write `current_proc.name[0]` to UART on every tick.

## Files to modify

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/vectors.S` — Replace slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) the same way slice 2.3 replaced slot 8: drop the panic macro for that slot and branch directly to `el0_64_irq_entry` (defined in the new `interrupt.S`). Maintain the 0x80-byte spacing. Slot 5 (`el1h_irq`) keeps the panic macro — kernel runs DAIF-masked, so an IRQ there is a bug.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` — Add `USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`. Allocate `USER_CODE_PAGE_B`, `USER_STACK_PAGE_B`, and (only if needed) `L3_CODE_TABLE_B`, `L3_STACK_TABLE_B` — both new VAs fall in the same 2 MiB L2 slot as the originals, so a single L3 covers all four pages. Map both code pages from `_user_stub_a_*` and `_user_stub_b_*` symbols. Add `STUB_PROC_NR_B = ProcNr::new(12)`; `userland_bootstrap` returns `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`. Change SPSR populated into each stub from `0x3C0` to `0x340` (clear `I` bit — unmask IRQs at EL0).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` — Generalize to two stub bodies. Either:
  1. Emit `_user_stub_a_start/end` and `_user_stub_b_start/end` as two `.rodata.user_stub` blobs with identical bodies (a tight `svc #0; b .start` loop — keeps the slice-2.3 SVC log alive for free), or
  2. Single blob, single pair of symbols; both stubs map the same page, identified only by `Proc::name`. Simpler — recommended.
  Both options keep the `svc #0` so the do_ipc log lines continue to appear, demonstrating that SVC and IRQ paths coexist.
- `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` — Add field `pub next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` (mirrors MINIX 3 `p_nextready` but as an index per `CLAUDE.md` convention). Add to `Proc::EMPTY = None`.
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` — Replace the current single-task `switch_to_user` body with a real scheduler:
  - Static `RUN_QUEUES: UnsafeCell<RunQueues>` where `RunQueues { head: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES], tail: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES] }` and `NR_SCHED_QUEUES = 16`. Same `Sync` newtype pattern as `ProcStorage` with documented single-threaded-boot invariant.
  - `enqueue(nr)` — append to tail of `head[p.priority as usize]`, mirror MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1605`.
  - `dequeue(nr)` — splice out by walking the list (queues stay tiny in slice 2.4).
  - `pick_proc() -> Option<ProcNr>` — scan queues 0..16, return first head; mirror MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1795`.
  - `reschedule()` — called from `clock::tick()` when the current proc's quantum expires: dequeue current, clear `RTS_NO_QUANTUM`, refill `quantum_left = quantum_ms`, enqueue at tail. Then update `CURRENT_PROC_NR` to `pick_proc()`'s result and write `tpidr_el1` to point at the new proc's `regs`. When `el1_return_to_user` runs at the bottom of the IRQ stub, it restores the *new* proc.
  - `switch_to_user(p)` becomes `run() -> !` — the first-entry path from `kmain`. Sets `CURRENT_PROC_NR`, enqueue both stubs (if not already), pick first, set tpidr_el1, jump to `el1_return_to_user`.
- `kernel/src/main.rs` — After `proc::init()` and `proc::dump_tables()`:
  1. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::gic::init(); }`
  2. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::timer::init(TICK_HZ); }`
  3. `let (a, b) = unsafe { arch::userland_bootstrap(); }`
  4. `unsafe { proc::sched::enqueue(a.nr); proc::sched::enqueue(b.nr); }`
  5. `unsafe { proc::sched::run(); }` — never returns.
- `kernel/build.rs` — Add `src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` to the sources array (line 21–26).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs` — `pub mod gic; pub mod timer; pub mod irq;` and re-export `userland_bootstrap` as `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`.
- `tools/qemu-run.sh` — Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly (cortex-a72 default is already GICv3 in modern QEMU, but make it explicit so the build isn't sensitive to QEMU's default-version drift).

## GICv3 init sequence (QEMU virt, single CPU)

1. **GICD** at HHDM + 0x0800_0000:
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← 0 (disable while configuring); wait for `RWP` bit (bit 31) to clear.
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← `EnableGrp1NS (bit 1) | ARE_NS (bit 4)`; wait RWP.
2. **GICR** for CPU 0 at HHDM + 0x080A_0000:
   - Clear `GICR_WAKER.ProcessorSleep` (bit 1); poll until `GICR_WAKER.ChildrenAsleep` (bit 2) clears.
3. **PPI 27 (virtual timer)** in GICR.SGI page (offset +0x10000):
   - `ICENABLER0` ← `1 << 27` (disable while configuring).
   - `IPRIORITYR[27]` ← `0x80`.
   - `ICFGR1` ← clear bits for intid 27 (level-triggered; PPIs are bits in ICFGR1).
   - `IGROUPR0` |= `1 << 27` (Group 1 non-secure).
   - `ISENABLER0` ← `1 << 27`.
4. **CPU interface** (ARMv8 system registers):
   - `ICC_SRE_EL1` |= 1 (system-register access).
   - `ICC_PMR_EL1` ← `0xFF` (allow all priorities).
   - `ICC_IGRPEN1_EL1` ← 1.
5. Final `isb` to ensure the system-register writes commit before any EL0 entry.

Reference for MMIO offsets: ARM IHI0069 (GICv3/GICv4 spec) §12 (GICD), §13 (GICR).

## Timer ISR data flow

```
EL0 stub running (DAIF.I clear)
  → CNTV_TVAL counts down to zero, CNTV asserts PPI 27
  → CPU takes exception, branches to vectors.S slot 9 → el0_64_irq_entry
  → save regs into tpidr_el1-pointed Proc.regs
  → bl do_irq(frame)
      → intid = gic::ack()
      → match intid {
          27 => {
              clock::tick();          // decrement quantum, print char, maybe reschedule
              timer::rearm();         // CNTV_TVAL ← period
          }
          1023 /* spurious */ => return,
          _ => panic
        }
      → gic::eoi(intid)
  → bl el1_return_to_user
      → loads regs from (possibly new) tpidr_el1
      → eret
```

`clock::tick()` semantics:

```rust
pub fn tick() {
    UPTIME.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    // SAFETY: IRQ handler runs with the current proc reference live; no other
    // mutable borrow into PROC_TABLE is held while we're in IRQ context.
    let cur = unsafe { proc::sched::current_proc_mut() }.expect("no current proc in tick");

    // Print the running proc's identifying char (visual proof of preemption).
    arch::uart_putchar(cur.name[0]);

    if cur.quantum_left > 0 {
        cur.quantum_left -= 1;
    }
    if cur.quantum_left == 0 {
        cur.rts_flags.fetch_or(RTS_NO_QUANTUM, Ordering::Relaxed);
        unsafe { proc::sched::reschedule(); }
    }
}
```

## Run-queue design (mirrors MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1605-1823`)

```rust
const NR_SCHED_QUEUES: usize = 16;

struct RunQueues {
    head: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
    tail: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
}

#[repr(transparent)]
struct RunQueueStorage(UnsafeCell<RunQueues>);
unsafe impl Sync for RunQueueStorage {}  // SAFETY: single-CPU; IRQ handler is the only async writer.

static RUNQ: RunQueueStorage = ...;
```

`Proc::next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` chains entries within a priority band. `enqueue` appends to tail; `dequeue` walks (acceptable — at most two entries in slice 2.4); `pick_proc` returns the head of the lowest-numbered non-empty queue.

## Verification

1. **Build clean.** `cargo kernel-aarch64 --release` — no warnings.
2. **Boot in QEMU.** `timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`. Expect the slice-2.3 banner / table dump first, then:
   ```
   entering EL0 stub tasks (preemption demo)...
   AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBBAAAA...
   ```
   - Approximately 100 chars/sec total (TICK_HZ = 100).
   - Quantum-grouped bursts (`SRV_QUANTUM_MS = 200` → 20-char bursts of each letter — adjust to ~5 ticks per quantum for compactness).
   - SVC log lines from `do_ipc` continue to interleave, demonstrating both SVC and IRQ paths work.
3. **Sanity-check preemption fairness.** Confirm both `A` and `B` appear in the output and neither monopolizes.
4. **Negative test.** Temporarily set `quantum_left = u64::MAX` on stub B; rebuild; confirm output is all `B`s (proves the scheduler actually consults the queue and that B was running first).
5. **Spurious-IRQ guard.** Boot with `-d int,cpu_reset -D qemu.log` and grep `qemu.log` for unexpected exception classes — only vector slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) entries should accumulate.

## Risks & open issues

- **QEMU GIC version default.** Modern QEMU defaults to GICv3 for cortex-a72, but `tools/qemu-run.sh` doesn't pin `gic-version`. Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly to harden against QEMU's version drift.
- **HHDM coverage of GIC bases.** `0x0800_0000` and `0x080A_0000` both fall inside Limine base-revision-2's `[0, 4 GiB)` blanket map — same regime as PL011 (slice 2.1 verified). The `gic.rs` constructor reads `arch::limine_hhdm_offset()` the same way `set_uart_base` does.
- **Kernel-task slots in run queue.** Solved by *not* enqueueing them at boot — only the two stub procs are enqueued. If a later slice needs `IDLE` as a fallback, it gets added then.
- **SPSR for first eret.** `populate_stub_slot` currently writes `spsr_el1 = 0x3C0` (all DAIF masked at EL0). Change to `0x340` so EL0 runs with IRQs unmasked. The kernel itself still runs DAIF-masked, so slot 5 (el1h_irq) remains a hard panic.
- **GICR per-CPU bringup ordering.** Single-CPU QEMU virt, so we only touch CPU 0's GICR; no MPIDR routing needed.

## Out of scope (defer)

- Real IPC primitives (slice 2.5).
- Kernel-call dispatch and `SYS_*` handlers (slice 2.6).
- Per-process address spaces / TTBR0 swap (Phase 3).
- ASIDs / TLBI sequencing beyond what TTBR0 bootstrap already does.
- SMP / per-CPU run queues.
- FIQ handling, SError introspection.
- Power management / wakeup sources beyond the timer.

```
  → CPU takes exception, branches to vectors.S slot 9 → el0_64_irq_entry
  → save regs into tpidr_el1-pointed Proc.regs
  → bl do_irq(frame)
      → intid = gic::ack()
      → match intid {
          27 => {
              clock::tick();          // decrement quantum, print char, maybe reschedule
              timer::rearm();         // CNTV_TVAL ← period
          }
          1023 /* spurious */ => return,
          _ => panic
        }
      → gic::eoi(intid)
  → bl el1_return_to_user
      → loads regs from (possibly new) tpidr_el1
      → eret
```

`clock::tick()` semantics:

```rust
pub fn tick() {
    UPTIME.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    // SAFETY: IRQ handler runs with the current proc reference live; no other
    // mutable borrow into PROC_TABLE is held while we're in IRQ context.
    let cur = unsafe { proc::sched::current_proc_mut() }.expect("no current proc in tick");

    // Print the running proc's identifying char (visual proof of preemption).
    arch::uart_putchar(cur.name[0]);

    if cur.quantum_left > 0 {
        cur.quantum_left -= 1;
    }
    if cur.quantum_left == 0 {
        cur.rts_flags.fetch_or(RTS_NO_QUANTUM, Ordering::Relaxed);
        unsafe { proc::sched::reschedule(); }
    }
}
```

## Run-queue design (mirrors MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1605-1823`)

```rust
const NR_SCHED_QUEUES: usize = 16;

struct RunQueues {
    head: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
    tail: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
}

#[repr(transparent)]
struct RunQueueStorage(UnsafeCell<RunQueues>);
unsafe impl Sync for RunQueueStorage {}  // SAFETY: single-CPU; IRQ handler is the only async writer.

static RUNQ: RunQueueStorage = ...;
```

`Proc::next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` chains entries within a priority band. `enqueue` appends to tail; `dequeue` walks (acceptable — at most two entries in slice 2.4); `pick_proc` returns the head of the lowest-numbered non-empty queue.

## Verification

1. **Build clean.** `cargo kernel-aarch64 --release` — no warnings.
2. **Boot in QEMU.** `timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`. Expect the slice-2.3 banner / table dump first, then:
   ```
   entering EL0 stub tasks (preemption demo)...
   AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBBAAAA...
   ```
   - Approximately 100 chars/sec total (TICK_HZ = 100).
   - Quantum-grouped bursts (`SRV_QUANTUM_MS = 200` → 20-char bursts of each letter — adjust to ~5 ticks per quantum for compactness).
   - SVC log lines from `do_ipc` continue to interleave, demonstrating both SVC and IRQ paths work.
3. **Sanity-check preemption fairness.** Confirm both `A` and `B` appear in the output and neither monopolizes.
4. **Negative test.** Temporarily set `quantum_left = u64::MAX` on stub B; rebuild; confirm output is all `B`s (proves the scheduler actually consults the queue and that B was running first).
5. **Spurious-IRQ guard.** Boot with `-d int,cpu_reset -D qemu.log` and grep `qemu.log` for unexpected exception classes — only vector slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) entries should accumulate.

## Risks & open issues

- **QEMU GIC version default.** Modern QEMU defaults to GICv3 for cortex-a72, but `tools/qemu-run.sh` doesn't pin `gic-version`. Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly to harden against QEMU's version drift.
- **HHDM coverage of GIC bases.** `0x0800_0000` and `0x080A_0000` both fall inside Limine base-revision-2's `[0, 4 GiB)` blanket map — same regime as PL011 (slice 2.1 verified). The `gic.rs` constructor reads `arch::limine_hhdm_offset()` the same way `set_uart_base` does.
- **Kernel-task slots in run queue.** Solved by *not* enqueueing them at boot — only the two stub procs are enqueued. If a later slice needs `IDLE` as a fallback, it gets added then.
- **SPSR for first eret.** `populate_stub_slot` currently writes `spsr_el1 = 0x3C0` (all DAIF masked at EL0). Change to `0x340` so EL0 runs with IRQs unmasked. The kernel itself still runs DAIF-masked, so slot 5 (el1h_irq) remains a hard panic.
- **GICR per-CPU bringup ordering.** Single-CPU QEMU virt, so we only touch CPU 0's GICR; no MPIDR routing needed.

## Out of scope (defer)

- Real IPC primitives (slice 2.5).
- Kernel-call dispatch and `SYS_*` handlers (slice 2.6).
- Per-process address spaces / TTBR0 swap (Phase 3).
- ASIDs / TLBI sequencing beyond what TTBR0 bootstrap already does.
- SMP / per-CPU run queues.
- FIQ handling, SError introspection.
- Power management / wakeup sources beyond the timer.

# Slice 2.4 — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues

## Context

Slice 2.3 shipped a single-task cooperative EL0 path: kmain → `userland_bootstrap` → `switch_to_user` erets into one EL0 stub that executes `svc #0` in a loop; each SVC lands in `do_ipc()`, logs to UART, returns to EL0. Phase 2's heart is preemptive scheduling, and slice 2.4 is the slice where time actually flows: the ARM generic timer raises PPI 27 through GICv3, the kernel ISR decrements the current task's quantum, and when the quantum hits zero the scheduler picks a different runnable task. Two EL0 stub tasks at the same priority will round-robin under preemption, and the kernel ISR prints one character per tick identifying the running task — output like `AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBB...` proves both tasks got CPU time and the timer drove the switches.

This satisfies the slice 2.4 milestone in `docs/plan.md:446`: *"Two EL0 stub tasks print interleaved bursts proving timer-driven preemption."* Slice 2.5 will replace the `svc #0` stub body with real IPC primitives; slice 2.6 will dispatch real kernel calls.

## Settled design decisions

- **Kernel-side per-tick logging.** EL0 stubs busy-loop (no MMIO from EL0). The kernel ISR writes one char from `current_proc.name[0]` to PL011 on each tick. Output groups in quantum-sized bursts because preemption only happens at quantum-exhaust.
- **Shared TTBR0, two VAs.** Extend the existing TTBR0 walk: task A keeps `USER_CODE_VA = 0x40_0000` / `USER_STACK_VA = 0x80_0000`, task B gets adjacent VAs (`USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`). No TTBR0_EL1 swap on context switch — Phase 3's VM server builds per-process address spaces.
- **No special idle handling.** If `pick_proc` returns `None`, the kernel halts in WFE with IRQs unmasked. Boot-image kernel-task slots (ASYNCM, IDLE, CLOCK, SYSTEM, HARDWARE) and server slots are *not* enqueued — they have no real EL0 context yet. Only the two stub slots enter the run queue.
- **Kernel runs DAIF-masked.** IRQs are unmasked only while EL0 runs (via SPSR bit clear). Slot 5 (el1h_irq) stays panic-on-trap for slice 2.4.

## Files to create

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/gic.rs` — GICv3 driver: HHDM-relative base resolution (`GICD = 0x0800_0000`, `GICR = 0x080A_0000` on QEMU virt cortex-a72), `init()` (distributor + redistributor wake + ICC_SRE/PMR/IGRPEN), `enable_ppi(intid, priority)`, `ack() -> u32` (ICC_IAR1_EL1), `eoi(intid)` (ICC_EOIR1_EL1).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/timer.rs` — ARM generic timer (virtual): `init(tick_hz)` reads `CNTFRQ_EL0`, programs `CNTV_TVAL_EL0`, sets `CNTV_CTL_EL0 = 1`; `rearm()` reloads TVAL each ISR; `INTID_VIRT_TIMER = 27` constant.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` — `el0_64_irq_entry`: save EL0 GPRs and SP_EL0/ELR/SPSR into `tpidr_el1`-pointed frame (mirrors `trap.S` idiom for `el0_64_sync_entry`), `bl do_irq`, tail-call `el1_return_to_user`. Added to `kernel/build.rs` sources.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/irq.rs` — `pub extern "C" fn do_irq(frame: &mut ArchRegisterFrame)`: ack via `gic::ack()`, dispatch by intid (PPI 27 → `clock::tick()`, else panic), eoi via `gic::eoi(intid)`. Returns; `el1_return_to_user` reads (possibly-new) `tpidr_el1`.
- `kernel/src/clock.rs` — arch-independent tick handler: increment `UPTIME` (`AtomicU64`); decrement `current_proc.quantum_left`; when it reaches 0, set `RTS_NO_QUANTUM` and call `sched::reschedule()`; write `current_proc.name[0]` to UART on every tick.

## Files to modify

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/vectors.S` — Replace slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) the same way slice 2.3 replaced slot 8: drop the panic macro for that slot and branch directly to `el0_64_irq_entry` (defined in the new `interrupt.S`). Maintain the 0x80-byte spacing. Slot 5 (`el1h_irq`) keeps the panic macro — kernel runs DAIF-masked, so an IRQ there is a bug.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` — Add `USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`. Allocate `USER_CODE_PAGE_B`, `USER_STACK_PAGE_B`, and (only if needed) `L3_CODE_TABLE_B`, `L3_STACK_TABLE_B` — both new VAs fall in the same 2 MiB L2 slot as the originals, so a single L3 covers all four pages. Map both code pages from `_user_stub_a_*` and `_user_stub_b_*` symbols. Add `STUB_PROC_NR_B = ProcNr::new(12)`; `userland_bootstrap` returns `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`. Change SPSR populated into each stub from `0x3C0` to `0x340` (clear `I` bit — unmask IRQs at EL0).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` — Generalize to two stub bodies. Either:
  1. Emit `_user_stub_a_start/end` and `_user_stub_b_start/end` as two `.rodata.user_stub` blobs with identical bodies (a tight `svc #0; b .start` loop — keeps the slice-2.3 SVC log alive for free), or
  2. Single blob, single pair of symbols; both stubs map the same page, identified only by `Proc::name`. Simpler — recommended.
  Both options keep the `svc #0` so the do_ipc log lines continue to appear, demonstrating that SVC and IRQ paths coexist.
- `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` — Add field `pub next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` (mirrors MINIX 3 `p_nextready` but as an index per `CLAUDE.md` convention). Add to `Proc::EMPTY = None`.
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` — Replace the current single-task `switch_to_user` body with a real scheduler:
  - Static `RUN_QUEUES: UnsafeCell<RunQueues>` where `RunQueues { head: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES], tail: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES] }` and `NR_SCHED_QUEUES = 16`. Same `Sync` newtype pattern as `ProcStorage` with documented single-threaded-boot invariant.
  - `enqueue(nr)` — append to tail of `head[p.priority as usize]`, mirror MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1605`.
  - `dequeue(nr)` — splice out by walking the list (queues stay tiny in slice 2.4).
  - `pick_proc() -> Option<ProcNr>` — scan queues 0..16, return first head; mirror MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1795`.
  - `reschedule()` — called from `clock::tick()` when the current proc's quantum expires: dequeue current, clear `RTS_NO_QUANTUM`, refill `quantum_left = quantum_ms`, enqueue at tail. Then update `CURRENT_PROC_NR` to `pick_proc()`'s result and write `tpidr_el1` to point at the new proc's `regs`. When `el1_return_to_user` runs at the bottom of the IRQ stub, it restores the *new* proc.
  - `switch_to_user(p)` becomes `run() -> !` — the first-entry path from `kmain`. Sets `CURRENT_PROC_NR`, enqueue both stubs (if not already), pick first, set tpidr_el1, jump to `el1_return_to_user`.
- `kernel/src/main.rs` — After `proc::init()` and `proc::dump_tables()`:
  1. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::gic::init(); }`
  2. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::timer::init(TICK_HZ); }`
  3. `let (a, b) = unsafe { arch::userland_bootstrap(); }`
  4. `unsafe { proc::sched::enqueue(a.nr); proc::sched::enqueue(b.nr); }`
  5. `unsafe { proc::sched::run(); }` — never returns.
- `kernel/build.rs` — Add `src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` to the sources array (line 21–26).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs` — `pub mod gic; pub mod timer; pub mod irq;` and re-export `userland_bootstrap` as `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`.
- `tools/qemu-run.sh` — Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly (cortex-a72 default is already GICv3 in modern QEMU, but make it explicit so the build isn't sensitive to QEMU's default-version drift).

## GICv3 init sequence (QEMU virt, single CPU)

1. **GICD** at HHDM + 0x0800_0000:
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← 0 (disable while configuring); wait for `RWP` bit (bit 31) to clear.
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← `EnableGrp1NS (bit 1) | ARE_NS (bit 4)`; wait RWP.
2. **GICR** for CPU 0 at HHDM + 0x080A_0000:
   - Clear `GICR_WAKER.ProcessorSleep` (bit 1); poll until `GICR_WAKER.ChildrenAsleep` (bit 2) clears.
3. **PPI 27 (virtual timer)** in GICR.SGI page (offset +0x10000):
   - `ICENABLER0` ← `1 << 27` (disable while configuring).
   - `IPRIORITYR[27]` ← `0x80`.
   - `ICFGR1` ← clear bits for intid 27 (level-triggered; PPIs are bits in ICFGR1).
   - `IGROUPR0` |= `1 << 27` (Group 1 non-secure).
   - `ISENABLER0` ← `1 << 27`.
4. **CPU interface** (ARMv8 system registers):
   - `ICC_SRE_EL1` |= 1 (system-register access).
   - `ICC_PMR_EL1` ← `0xFF` (allow all priorities).
   - `ICC_IGRPEN1_EL1` ← 1.
5. Final `isb` to ensure the system-register writes commit before any EL0 entry.

Reference for MMIO offsets: ARM IHI0069 (GICv3/GICv4 spec) §12 (GICD), §13 (GICR).

## Timer ISR data flow

```

  → CNTV_TVAL counts down to zero, CNTV asserts PPI 27
  → CPU takes exception, branches to vectors.S slot 9 → el0_64_irq_entry

  → bl do_irq(frame)
      → intid = gic::ack()
      → match intid {
          27 => {

              timer::rearm();         // CNTV_TVAL ← period
          }
          1023 /* spurious */ => return,
          _ => panic
        }
      → gic::eoi(intid)
  → bl el1_return_to_user
      → loads regs from (possibly new) tpidr_el1
      → eret
```

```rust

    }

        cur.rts_flags.fetch_or(RTS_NO_QUANTUM, Ordering::Relaxed);

}
```

  - `dequeue(nr)` — splice out by walking the list (queues stay tiny in slice 2.4).
  - `pick_proc() -> Option<ProcNr>` — scan queues 0..16, return first head; mirror MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1795`.
  - `reschedule()` — called from `clock::tick()` when the current proc's quantum expires: dequeue current, clear `RTS_NO_QUANTUM`, refill `quantum_left = quantum_ms`, enqueue at tail. Then update `CURRENT_PROC_NR` to `pick_proc()`'s result and write `tpidr_el1` to point at the new proc's `regs`. When `el1_return_to_user` runs at the bottom of the IRQ stub, it restores the *new* proc.
  - `switch_to_user(p)` becomes `run() -> !` — the first-entry path from `kmain`. Sets `CURRENT_PROC_NR`, enqueue both stubs (if not already), pick first, set tpidr_el1, jump to `el1_return_to_user`.
- `kernel/src/main.rs` — After `proc::init()` and `proc::dump_tables()`:
  1. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::gic::init(); }`
  2. `unsafe { arch::aarch64::timer::init(TICK_HZ); }`
  3. `let (a, b) = unsafe { arch::userland_bootstrap(); }`
  4. `unsafe { proc::sched::enqueue(a.nr); proc::sched::enqueue(b.nr); }`
  5. `unsafe { proc::sched::run(); }` — never returns.
- `kernel/build.rs` — Add `src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` to the sources array (line 21–26).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs` — `pub mod gic; pub mod timer; pub mod irq;` and re-export `userland_bootstrap` as `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`.
- `tools/qemu-run.sh` — Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly (cortex-a72 default is already GICv3 in modern QEMU, but make it explicit so the build isn't sensitive to QEMU's default-version drift).

## GICv3 init sequence (QEMU virt, single CPU)

1. **GICD** at HHDM + 0x0800_0000:
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← 0 (disable while configuring); wait for `RWP` bit (bit 31) to clear.
   - `GICD_CTLR` ← `EnableGrp1NS (bit 1) | ARE_NS (bit 4)`; wait RWP.
2. **GICR** for CPU 0 at HHDM + 0x080A_0000:
   - Clear `GICR_WAKER.ProcessorSleep` (bit 1); poll until `GICR_WAKER.ChildrenAsleep` (bit 2) clears.
3. **PPI 27 (virtual timer)** in GICR.SGI page (offset +0x10000):
   - `ICENABLER0` ← `1 << 27` (disable while configuring).
   - `IPRIORITYR[27]` ← `0x80`.
   - `ICFGR1` ← clear bits for intid 27 (level-triggered; PPIs are bits in ICFGR1).
   - `IGROUPR0` |= `1 << 27` (Group 1 non-secure).
   - `ISENABLER0` ← `1 << 27`.
4. **CPU interface** (ARMv8 system registers):
   - `ICC_SRE_EL1` |= 1 (system-register access).
   - `ICC_PMR_EL1` ← `0xFF` (allow all priorities).
   - `ICC_IGRPEN1_EL1` ← 1.
5. Final `isb` to ensure the system-register writes commit before any EL0 entry.

Reference for MMIO offsets: ARM IHI0069 (GICv3/GICv4 spec) §12 (GICD), §13 (GICR).

## Timer ISR data flow

```
EL0 stub running (DAIF.I clear)
  → CNTV_TVAL counts down to zero, CNTV asserts PPI 27
  → CPU takes exception, branches to vectors.S slot 9 → el0_64_irq_entry
  → save regs into tpidr_el1-pointed Proc.regs
  → bl do_irq(frame)
      → intid = gic::ack()
      → match intid {
          27 => {
              clock::tick();          // decrement quantum, print char, maybe reschedule
              timer::rearm();         // CNTV_TVAL ← period
          }
          1023 /* spurious */ => return,
          _ => panic
        }
      → gic::eoi(intid)
  → bl el1_return_to_user
      → loads regs from (possibly new) tpidr_el1
      → eret
```

`clock::tick()` semantics:

```rust
pub fn tick() {
    UPTIME.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    // SAFETY: IRQ handler runs with the current proc reference live; no other
    // mutable borrow into PROC_TABLE is held while we're in IRQ context.
    let cur = unsafe { proc::sched::current_proc_mut() }.expect("no current proc in tick");

    // Print the running proc's identifying char (visual proof of preemption).
    arch::uart_putchar(cur.name[0]);

    if cur.quantum_left > 0 {
        cur.quantum_left -= 1;
    }
    if cur.quantum_left == 0 {
        cur.rts_flags.fetch_or(RTS_NO_QUANTUM, Ordering::Relaxed);
        unsafe { proc::sched::reschedule(); }
    }
}
```

## Run-queue design (mirrors MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.c:1605-1823`)

```rust
const NR_SCHED_QUEUES: usize = 16;

struct RunQueues {
    head: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
    tail: [Option<ProcNr>; NR_SCHED_QUEUES],
}

#[repr(transparent)]
struct RunQueueStorage(UnsafeCell<RunQueues>);
unsafe impl Sync for RunQueueStorage {}  // SAFETY: single-CPU; IRQ handler is the only async writer.

static RUNQ: RunQueueStorage = ...;
```

`Proc::next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` chains entries within a priority band. `enqueue` appends to tail; `dequeue` walks (acceptable — at most two entries in slice 2.4); `pick_proc` returns the head of the lowest-numbered non-empty queue.

## Verification

1. **Build clean.** `cargo kernel-aarch64 --release` — no warnings.
2. **Boot in QEMU.** `timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`. Expect the slice-2.3 banner / table dump first, then:
   ```
   entering EL0 stub tasks (preemption demo)...
   AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBBAAAA...
   ```
   - Approximately 100 chars/sec total (TICK_HZ = 100).
   - Quantum-grouped bursts (`SRV_QUANTUM_MS = 200` → 20-char bursts of each letter — adjust to ~5 ticks per quantum for compactness).
   - SVC log lines from `do_ipc` continue to interleave, demonstrating both SVC and IRQ paths work.
3. **Sanity-check preemption fairness.** Confirm both `A` and `B` appear in the output and neither monopolizes.
4. **Negative test.** Temporarily set `quantum_left = u64::MAX` on stub B; rebuild; confirm output is all `B`s (proves the scheduler actually consults the queue and that B was running first).
5. **Spurious-IRQ guard.** Boot with `-d int,cpu_reset -D qemu.log` and grep `qemu.log` for unexpected exception classes — only vector slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) entries should accumulate.

## Risks & open issues

- **QEMU GIC version default.** Modern QEMU defaults to GICv3 for cortex-a72, but `tools/qemu-run.sh` doesn't pin `gic-version`. Add `-machine virt,gic-version=3` explicitly to harden against QEMU's version drift.
- **HHDM coverage of GIC bases.** `0x0800_0000` and `0x080A_0000` both fall inside Limine base-revision-2's `[0, 4 GiB)` blanket map — same regime as PL011 (slice 2.1 verified). The `gic.rs` constructor reads `arch::limine_hhdm_offset()` the same way `set_uart_base` does.
- **Kernel-task slots in run queue.** Solved by *not* enqueueing them at boot — only the two stub procs are enqueued. If a later slice needs `IDLE` as a fallback, it gets added then.
- **SPSR for first eret.** `populate_stub_slot` currently writes `spsr_el1 = 0x3C0` (all DAIF masked at EL0). Change to `0x340` so EL0 runs with IRQs unmasked. The kernel itself still runs DAIF-masked, so slot 5 (el1h_irq) remains a hard panic.
- **GICR per-CPU bringup ordering.** Single-CPU QEMU virt, so we only touch CPU 0's GICR; no MPIDR routing needed.

## Out of scope (defer)

- Real IPC primitives (slice 2.5).
- Kernel-call dispatch and `SYS_*` handlers (slice 2.6).
- Per-process address spaces / TTBR0 swap (Phase 3).
- ASIDs / TLBI sequencing beyond what TTBR0 bootstrap already does.
- SMP / per-CPU run queues.
- FIQ handling, SError introspection.
- Power management / wakeup sources beyond the timer.

toolUseResult.plan:
# Slice 2.4 — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues

## Context

Slice 2.3 shipped a single-task cooperative EL0 path: kmain → `userland_bootstrap` → `switch_to_user` erets into one EL0 stub that executes `svc #0` in a loop; each SVC lands in `do_ipc()`, logs to UART, returns to EL0. Phase 2's heart is preemptive scheduling, and slice 2.4 is the slice where time actually flows: the ARM generic timer raises PPI 27 through GICv3, the kernel ISR decrements the current task's quantum, and when the quantum hits zero the scheduler picks a different runnable task. Two EL0 stub tasks at the same priority will round-robin under preemption, and the kernel ISR prints one character per tick identifying the running task — output like `AAAAABBBBBAAAAABBBBB...` proves both tasks got CPU time and the timer drove the switches.

This satisfies the slice 2.4 milestone in `docs/plan.md:446`: *"Two EL0 stub tasks print interleaved bursts proving timer-driven preemption."* Slice 2.5 will replace the `svc #0` stub body with real IPC primitives; slice 2.6 will dispatch real kernel calls.

## Settled design decisions

- **Kernel-side per-tick logging.** EL0 stubs busy-loop (no MMIO from EL0). The kernel ISR writes one char from `current_proc.name[0]` to PL011 on each tick. Output groups in quantum-sized bursts because preemption only happens at quantum-exhaust.
- **Shared TTBR0, two VAs.** Extend the existing TTBR0 walk: task A keeps `USER_CODE_VA = 0x40_0000` / `USER_STACK_VA = 0x80_0000`, task B gets adjacent VAs (`USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`). No TTBR0_EL1 swap on context switch — Phase 3's VM server builds per-process address spaces.
- **No special idle handling.** If `pick_proc` returns `None`, the kernel halts in WFE with IRQs unmasked. Boot-image kernel-task slots (ASYNCM, IDLE, CLOCK, SYSTEM, HARDWARE) and server slots are *not* enqueued — they have no real EL0 context yet. Only the two stub slots enter the run queue.
- **Kernel runs DAIF-masked.** IRQs are unmasked only while EL0 runs (via SPSR bit clear). Slot 5 (el1h_irq) stays panic-on-trap for slice 2.4.

## Files to create

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/gic.rs` — GICv3 driver: HHDM-relative base resolution (`GICD = 0x0800_0000`, `GICR = 0x080A_0000` on QEMU virt cortex-a72), `init()` (distributor + redistributor wake + ICC_SRE/PMR/IGRPEN), `enable_ppi(intid, priority)`, `ack() -> u32` (ICC_IAR1_EL1), `eoi(intid)` (ICC_EOIR1_EL1).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/timer.rs` — ARM generic timer (virtual): `init(tick_hz)` reads `CNTFRQ_EL0`, programs `CNTV_TVAL_EL0`, sets `CNTV_CTL_EL0 = 1`; `rearm()` reloads TVAL each ISR; `INTID_VIRT_TIMER = 27` constant.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` — `el0_64_irq_entry`: save EL0 GPRs and SP_EL0/ELR/SPSR into `tpidr_el1`-pointed frame (mirrors `trap.S` idiom for `el0_64_sync_entry`), `bl do_irq`, tail-call `el1_return_to_user`. Added to `kernel/build.rs` sources.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/irq.rs` — `pub extern "C" fn do_irq(frame: &mut ArchRegisterFrame)`: ack via `gic::ack()`, dispatch by intid (PPI 27 → `clock::tick()`, else panic), eoi via `gic::eoi(intid)`. Returns; `el1_return_to_user` reads (possibly-new) `tpidr_el1`.
- `kernel/src/clock.rs` — arch-independent tick handler: increment `UPTIME` (`AtomicU64`); decrement `current_proc.quantum_left`; when it reaches 0, set `RTS_NO_QUANTUM` and call `sched::reschedule()`; write `current_proc.name[0]` to UART on every tick.

## Files to modify

- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/vectors.S` — Replace slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) the same way slice 2.3 replaced slot 8: drop the panic macro for that slot and branch directly to `el0_64_irq_entry` (defined in the new `interrupt.S`). Maintain the 0x80-byte spacing. Slot 5 (`el1h_irq`) keeps the panic macro — kernel runs DAIF-masked, so an IRQ there is a bug.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` — Add `USER_CODE_VA_B = 0x41_0000`, `USER_STACK_VA_B = 0x81_0000`. Allocate `USER_CODE_PAGE_B`, `USER_STACK_PAGE_B`, and (only if needed) `L3_CODE_TABLE_B`, `L3_STACK_TABLE_B` — both new VAs fall in the same 2 MiB L2 slot as the originals, so a single L3 covers all four pages. Map both code pages from `_user_stub_a_*` and `_user_stub_b_*` symbols. Add `STUB_PROC_NR_B = ProcNr::new(12)`; `userland_bootstrap` returns `(&mut Proc, &mut Proc)`. Change SPSR populated into each stub from `0x3C0` to `0x340` (clear `I` bit — unmask IRQs at EL0).
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` — Generalize to two stub bodies. Either:
  1. Emit `_user_stub_a_start/end` and `_user_stub_b_start/end` as two `.rodata.user_stub` blobs with identical bodies (a tight `svc #0; b .start` loop — keeps the slice-2.3 SVC log alive for free), or
  2. Single blob, single pair of symbols; both stubs map the same page, identified only by `Proc::name`. Simpler — recommended.

When `el1_return_to_user` runs at the bottom of the IRQ stub, it restores the *new* proc.

input.content:
/*
 * MINIX 4 aarch64 EL0 → EL1 IRQ entry stub.
 *
 * Wired in `vectors.S` vector slot 9 (`el0_64_irq`) — the IRQ vector for an
 * exception taken from a Lower EL using AArch64. Mirrors the save/restore
 * idiom from `el0_64_sync_entry` (slice 2.3): user state goes into the
 * `tpidr_el1`-pointed `ArchRegisterFrame`, the Rust dispatcher runs with
 * the frame pointer in x0, then the tail returns to whichever EL0 context
 * `tpidr_el1` still points at — possibly a *different* proc if `do_irq`
 * rescheduled.
 *
 * On entry:
 *   - EL1h, SP = SP_EL1 (kernel stack).
 *   - All EL0 user regs x0..x30 hold caller values, unsaved.
 *   - TPIDR_EL1 = &mut CURRENT_PROC->regs.
 *   - PSTATE.DAIF: I bit set (IRQs masked while we handle this one).
 *   - ELR_EL1 / SPSR_EL1 describe the interrupted EL0 context.
 *
 * Field offsets must match `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/context.rs`. See the
 * matching .equ block in `trap.S`; we duplicate them rather than #include
 * to keep each .S file self-contained for the cc-crate-style build.
 */

    .equ REGS_X_OFFSET,      0
    .equ REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET, 248
    .equ REGS_ELR_OFFSET,    256
    .equ REGS_SPSR_OFFSET,   264

    .section .text.interrupt, "ax", %progbits

/*
 * el0_64_irq_entry — entry stub for vector slot 9 (EL0 AArch64 IRQ).
 *
 * Same calling convention as el0_64_sync_entry: spill the user's x29/x30 to
 * the kernel stack to free two scratch registers, load the frame pointer
 * from TPIDR_EL1 into x29, save x0..x28 directly, then recover x29/x30 from
 * the kernel stack and store into the frame. Finally save SP_EL0, ELR_EL1,
 * SPSR_EL1 and tail-call do_irq.
 */
    .global el0_64_irq_entry
    .type el0_64_irq_entry, %function
el0_64_irq_entry:
    // Free x29/x30 for scratch use.
    stp     x29, x30, [sp, #-16]!

    // x29 = &frame.
    mrs     x29, tpidr_el1

    // Save x0..x28 into the frame.
    stp     x0,  x1,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
    stp     x2,  x3,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
    stp     x4,  x5,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
    stp     x6,  x7,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
    stp     x8,  x9,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
    stp     x10, x11, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
    stp     x12, x13, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
    stp     x14, x15, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
    stp     x18, x19, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
    stp     x20, x21, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
    stp     x22, x23, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
    stp     x24, x25, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
    stp     x26, x27, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
    str     x28,      [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]

    // Pop the user's x29/x30 off the kernel stack and store into the frame.
    ldp     x16, x17, [sp], #16
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE8]

    // Save SP_EL0, ELR_EL1, SPSR_EL1.
    mrs     x9,  sp_el0
    mrs     x10, elr_el1
    mrs     x11, spsr_el1
    stp     x9,  x10, [x29, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]
    str     x11,      [x29, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]

    // do_irq(&mut frame). May update tpidr_el1 if a context switch happens.
    mov     x0, x29
    bl      do_irq

    // Tail-call the shared eret stub from trap.S. It re-reads tpidr_el1, so
    // a reschedule inside do_irq is picked up here automatically.
    b       el1_return_to_user
    .size el0_64_irq_entry, . - el0_64_irq_entry

toolUseResult.originalFile:
/*
 * MINIX 4 aarch64 exception vector table.
 *
 * Layout per ARMv8 ARM D1.10: 16 entries of 128 bytes each, table aligned
 * to 2 KiB. Four groups of four entries:
 *   - Current EL with SP_EL0
 *   - Current EL with SP_ELx
 *   - Lower EL using AArch64
 *   - Lower EL using AArch32
 * Each group has: Synchronous, IRQ, FIQ, SError.
 *
 * Phase 1 policy: every exception is fatal. Each entry saves the integer
 * register file plus the relevant system registers onto SP and calls into
 * `exception_entry`, which panics. We don't expect any exceptions yet --
 * the kernel just prints a banner and halts -- so this exists solely to
 * make any inadvertent fault legible instead of silently rebooting.
 *
 * Frame layout (matches ExceptionFrame in exception.rs):
 *   offset  field
 *   0x000   x0..x30 (31 * 8 bytes = 0xF8)
 *   0x0F8   SPSR_EL1
 *   0x100   ELR_EL1
 *   0x108   ESR_EL1
 *   0x110   FAR_EL1
 *   0x118   pad
 * Total: 0x120 bytes.
 */

    .macro EXC_ENTRY name, kind
    .global \name
    .type \name, %function
\name:
    sub     sp, sp, #0x120
    stp     x0,  x1,  [sp, #0x000]
    stp     x2,  x3,  [sp, #0x010]
    stp     x4,  x5,  [sp, #0x020]
    stp     x6,  x7,  [sp, #0x030]
    stp     x8,  x9,  [sp, #0x040]
    stp     x10, x11, [sp, #0x050]
    stp     x12, x13, [sp, #0x060]
    stp     x14, x15, [sp, #0x070]
    stp     x16, x17, [sp, #0x080]
    stp     x18, x19, [sp, #0x090]
    stp     x20, x21, [sp, #0x0A0]
    stp     x22, x23, [sp, #0x0B0]
    stp     x24, x25, [sp, #0x0C0]
    stp     x26, x27, [sp, #0x0D0]
    stp     x28, x29, [sp, #0x0E0]
    str     x30,      [sp, #0x0F0]
    mrs     x9,  spsr_el1
    mrs     x10, elr_el1
    mrs     x11, esr_el1
    mrs     x12, far_el1
    stp     x9,  x10, [sp, #0x0F8]
    stp     x11, x12, [sp, #0x108]
    mov     x0, sp                  // arg 0: pointer to frame
    mov     x1, #\kind              // arg 1: vector index 0..15
    bl      exception_entry
1:  wfe
    b       1b
    .size \name, . - \name
    .endm

    .section .text.vectors, "ax", %progbits
    .balign 0x800
    .global _vector_table
_vector_table:
    // Current EL with SP_EL0
    EXC_ENTRY el1t_sync,    0
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1t_irq,     1
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1t_fiq,     2
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1t_serror,  3
    // Current EL with SP_ELx
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1h_sync,    4
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1h_irq,     5
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1h_fiq,     6
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el1h_serror,  7
    // Lower EL using AArch64
    //
    // Slot 8 (synchronous EL0 trap) is the SVC entry point — slice 2.3
    // wires it to `el0_64_sync_entry` in `trap.S` instead of the generic
    // panic macro so EL0 IPC traps land in the SVC handler rather than
    // dumping registers and crashing. The .balign before the next entry
    // keeps the per-vector 128-byte spacing intact.
    .balign 0x80
    .global el0_64_sync
    .type el0_64_sync, %function
el0_64_sync:
    b       el0_64_sync_entry
    .size el0_64_sync, . - el0_64_sync
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_64_irq,     9
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_64_fiq,    10
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_64_serror, 11
    // Lower EL using AArch32
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_32_sync,   12
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_32_irq,    13
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_32_fiq,    14
    .balign 0x80
    EXC_ENTRY el0_32_serror, 15
    .size _vector_table, . - _vector_table

1	//! Minimal MMU helpers for the slice-2.3 EL0 stub task.
2	//!
3	//! Slice 2.3 needs exactly two user mappings — one code page and one stack
4	//! page — installed via TTBR0_EL1. Phase 3's VM server takes this over and
5	//! replaces it with proper per-process address spaces; for now we hand-roll
6	//! a tiny 4 KiB-granule, 4-level page-table walk in static `.bss` storage.
7	//!
8	//! Layout decisions:
9	//! - 4 KiB granule, 4 levels, T0SZ = 16 (48-bit VA). Symmetric with the
10	//!   TTBR1 settings Limine programmed for the higher-half kernel; using the
11	//!   same granule for TTBR0 lets us reuse Limine's MAIR indices unchanged.
12	//! - We never write `MAIR_EL1` — Limine has already placed `Normal WB` at
13	//!   AttrIdx 0. We read once at boot and assert that invariant.
14	//! - We touch only the `*0` field-set bits of `TCR_EL1` (T0SZ / IRGN0 /
15	//!   ORGN0 / SH0 / TG0 / EPD0) — the `*1` fields govern TTBR1, which is the
16	//!   kernel's own translation regime and must not be perturbed.
17	
18	use core::arch::asm;
19	
20	/// 4 KiB page size, in bytes.
21	pub const PAGE_SIZE: usize = 4096;
22	/// log2 of [`PAGE_SIZE`].
23	pub const PAGE_SHIFT: u32 = 12;
24	/// 512 entries per 4 KiB page table.
25	pub const PTES_PER_LEVEL: usize = 512;
26	
27	// ----- PTE bit positions ----------------------------------------------------
28	//
29	// ARMv8 stage-1 descriptor format (D5.3 in the ARM ARM). The bits we use:
30	
31	/// Entry is valid. Cleared = "translation fault on access".
32	pub const PTE_VALID: u64 = 1 << 0;
33	/// At L0/L1/L2: entry points at a next-level table (vs. a block). At L3:
34	/// must be 1 to indicate a page descriptor (block descriptors don't exist
35	/// at L3).
36	pub const PTE_TABLE: u64 = 1 << 1;
37	/// Inner Shareable shareability attribute. Mandatory for cacheable mappings
38	/// on multi-core systems; harmless on single-core QEMU.
39	pub const PTE_SH_INNER: u64 = 0b11 << 8;
40	/// Access Flag — must be set, or the first access generates a fault.
41	pub const PTE_AF: u64 = 1 << 10;
42	/// Privileged eXecute Never — kernel cannot fetch instructions from this
43	/// page. Always set on user mappings.
44	pub const PTE_PXN: u64 = 1 << 53;
45	/// Unprivileged eXecute Never — EL0 cannot fetch instructions. Set on
46	/// data/stack pages; cleared on code pages.
47	pub const PTE_UXN: u64 = 1 << 54;
48	
49	/// Access-permission field selecting EL1 RW + EL0 RW.
50	pub const PTE_AP_RW_EL0: u64 = 0b01 << 6;
51	/// Access-permission field selecting EL1 RO + EL0 RO.
52	pub const PTE_AP_RO_EL0: u64 = 0b11 << 6;
53	
54	/// Pack a MAIR attribute index into the descriptor's AttrIndx field.
55	pub const fn pte_attr_idx(idx: u64) -> u64 {
56	    (idx & 0b111) << 2
57	}
58	
59	/// MAIR_EL1 AttrIdx 0 — must encode Normal memory, Write-Back, RAWA. Limine
60	/// programs this; slice 2.3 only verifies.
61	pub const ATTR_IDX_NORMAL: u64 = 0;
62	/// Expected MAIR_EL1[7:0] for AttrIdx 0.
63	pub const MAIR_NORMAL_BYTE: u8 = 0xFF;
64	
65	// ----- PageTable -----------------------------------------------------------
66	
67	/// One 4 KiB page table — 512 × 8 B descriptors. The structure is its own
68	/// alignment guarantee so we can place it in static storage and hand a raw
69	/// physical address straight to TTBR0.
70	#[repr(C, align(4096))]
71	pub struct PageTable(pub [u64; PTES_PER_LEVEL]);
72	
73	impl PageTable {
74	    /// All-zero (= all-invalid) table for static initialization.
75	    pub const EMPTY: Self = Self([0; PTES_PER_LEVEL]);
76	
77	    #[inline]
78	    pub fn set(&mut self, idx: usize, desc: u64) {
79	        self.0[idx] = desc;
80	    }

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# tools/qemu-run.sh -- cargo runner for the aarch64 kernel target.
#
# Cargo invokes this with the kernel ELF path as $1. We build a tiny ESP
# directory tree on the fly and hand it to QEMU's `-drive file=fat:rw:DIR`
# helper, which exposes the directory as a FAT32 filesystem. That avoids
# needing parted/mtools/hdiutil to land a "MINIX 4 booting" banner.
#
# Override the UEFI firmware location with QEMU_EFI_AARCH64=/path/to/fw.fd.

set -euo pipefail

if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "usage: $0 <kernel-elf> [extra qemu args...]" >&2
    exit 64
fi

KERNEL="$1"
shift

ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
ESP="$ROOT/target/esp"
LIMINE_BIN="$ROOT/external/limine/dist/BOOTAA64.EFI"

# Materialize the Limine binary if `make` hasn't been run yet.
if [[ ! -f "$LIMINE_BIN" ]]; then
    echo "==> Fetching Limine binaries (one-time)..." >&2
    make -C "$ROOT/external/limine" >&2
fi

# Locate aarch64 UEFI firmware. Honor explicit override first.
FIRMWARE_CANDIDATES=(
    "${QEMU_EFI_AARCH64:-}"
    "$(brew --prefix qemu 2>/dev/null || true)/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/opt/homebrew/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/usr/local/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd"
    "/usr/share/edk2-aarch64/QEMU_EFI.fd"
    "/usr/share/AAVMF/AAVMF_CODE.fd"
)
FIRMWARE=""
for cand in "${FIRMWARE_CANDIDATES[@]}"; do
    if [[ -n "$cand" && -f "$cand" ]]; then
        FIRMWARE="$cand"
        break
    fi
done
if [[ -z "$FIRMWARE" ]]; then
    cat >&2 <<'EOF'
error: no aarch64 UEFI firmware found.
       Install QEMU (brew install qemu) or point QEMU_EFI_AARCH64 at an
       OVMF/edk2 firmware blob, e.g.:
           export QEMU_EFI_AARCH64=/path/to/QEMU_EFI.fd
EOF
    exit 69
fi

# Stage the ESP directory tree.
rm -rf "$ESP"
mkdir -p "$ESP/EFI/BOOT" "$ESP/boot"
cp "$LIMINE_BIN" "$ESP/EFI/BOOT/BOOTAA64.EFI"
cp "$ROOT/tools/limine.conf" "$ESP/limine.conf"
cp "$KERNEL" "$ESP/boot/kernel"

exec qemu-system-aarch64 \
    -M virt -cpu cortex-a72 -m 256M \
    -bios "$FIRMWARE" \
    -drive "file=fat:rw:fat-type=32:$ESP,format=raw,if=virtio" \
    -display none \
    -serial stdio \
    -no-reboot \
    "$@"

# Cargo configuration for MINIX 4
#
# The kernel builds with a custom target spec (no_std, no_main, kernel code model).
# Servers and userland build with a freestanding user-space target.

[build]
# Default target for `cargo build` -- set per-crate via .cargo/config.toml
# or override with --target on command line.

[target.aarch64-unknown-none]
runner = "tools/qemu-run.sh"
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=-Tkernel/src/arch/aarch64/linker.ld"]

[target.x86_64-unknown-none]
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=-Tkernel/src/arch/x86_64/linker.ld"]

# Aliases for common development commands
[alias]
kernel-aarch64 = "build -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release"
kernel-x86_64 = "build -p minix4-kernel --target x86_64-unknown-none --release"
---
#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
pub mod aarch64;
#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
pub use aarch64::*;

#[cfg(not(target_arch = "aarch64"))]
compile_error!("MINIX 4 currently supports only aarch64; x86_64 arrives in Phase 8");

Let me capture the demo evidence, update docs/plan.md, and finish up.

content.subject: Create feature branch and stash plan.md tweak

content.subject: Add next_ready field to Proc struct
content.description: Add Option<ProcNr> next_ready field for run-queue linkage; update Proc::EMPTY.

content.subject: Build run-queue scheduler in proc::sched
content.description: RUNQ static, enqueue/dequeue/pick_proc, reschedule, run(), expanded current_proc_mut.

content.subject: Add clock.rs tick handler
content.description: UPTIME atomic counter, per-tick char print, quantum decrement, RTS_NO_QUANTUM trigger, reschedule call.

content.subject: Add aarch64 gic.rs (GICv3 driver)
content.description: GICD/GICR base resolution via HHDM; init() sequence; enable_ppi; ack/eoi via ICC_*_EL1.

content.subject: Add aarch64 timer.rs (CNTV setup)
content.description: init(tick_hz), rearm(); CNTFRQ_EL0 read, CNTV_TVAL_EL0 + CNTV_CTL_EL0 writes; INTID_VIRT_TIMER constant.

content.subject: Add aarch64 interrupt.S + irq.rs
content.description: el0_64_irq_entry: save EL0 state into tpidr_el1 frame, bl do_irq, tail-call el1_return_to_user. do_irq: ack, dispatch, eoi.

content.subject: Wire vectors.S slot 9 to interrupt entry
content.description: Replace el0_64_irq panic macro with direct branch to el0_64_irq_entry (mirrors slice-2.3 treatment of slot 8).

content.subject: Extend userland.rs for two stub tasks
content.description: USER_CODE_VA_B / USER_STACK_VA_B, second pair of pages, STUB_PROC_NR_B; return both procs; SPSR unmasks I.

content.subject: Update build.rs and qemu-run.sh
content.description: Assemble interrupt.S; pin -machine virt,gic-version=3 in qemu-run.sh.
content.activeForm: Updating build script and qemu runner

content.subject: Wire kmain to new boot sequence
content.description: After proc::init: gic::init, timer::init, userland_bootstrap returns two procs, enqueue both, sched::run().

content.content:
420	  `RTS_NO_PRIV`, contiguous priv-ids 0–15). `kernel-shared` migrated
421	  `ProcNr` / `PrivId` / `SysId` from `i32` aliases to newtypes,
422	  added `NR_SYS_CALLS = 32` and a new `sys_limits` module
423	  (`NR_IO_RANGE`, `NR_IRQ`, `NR_MEM_RANGE`). `ArchRegisterFrame` stub
424	  in `arch/aarch64/context.rs` ready for slice 2.3 to populate. 25
425	  host-side tests pass (21 existing + 4 new newtype round-trips);
426	  clean release build with no warnings.
427	- **Slice 2.3** ✓ shipped (PR #5, merged 2026-05-22) — aarch64 SVC entry
428	  cooperative context switch. `entry.S`
429	  promotes the kernel from Limine's EL1t to EL1h on a primed SP_EL1
430	  (`mov x9, sp; msr SPSel, #1; mov sp, x9` — the only sequence that
431	  worked on QEMU virt / Cortex-A72; a bare `msr sp_el1, xN` from EL1t
432	  silently locks the core). New `arch/aarch64/{mmu,userland,trap,
433	  user_stub}.S/rs` build a minimal TTBR0_EL1 walk that maps one EL0
434	  code page (RO+X) and one EL0 stack page (RW+NX) at low VAs, copies a
435	  hand-coded `svc #0; b .` stub blob into the code page, and erets into
436	  EL0. Vector slot 8 (`el0_64_sync`) is specialized to `b
437	  el0_64_sync_entry` in `trap.S`; the SVC handler parks `&mut p.regs`
438	  in `TPIDR_EL1` so it can save state directly into the proc table and
439	  tail-call back to EL0 via `el1_return_to_user`. `do_ipc(frame)` in
440	  the new `kernel/src/ipc/` module prints each call and stores `OK` in
441	  `frame.x[0]`. `proc::sched::switch_to_user` wraps the eret. Verified
442	  in QEMU: `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 src_dst=32766 msg=0x0...` repeats
443	  ~16K times per second.
444	- **Slice 2.4** ◀ next — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues. Two
445	  EL0 stub tasks print interleaved bursts proving timer-driven
446	  preemption.
447	- **Slice 2.5** — IPC primitives (`mini_send`, `mini_receive`,
448	  `mini_notify`, `mini_senda`, `mini_sendnb`, `deadlock_check`). Two
449	  tasks ping-pong messages. **This is the Phase 2 milestone.**
450	- **Slice 2.6** — Kernel-call dispatch and the minimum `SYS_*` set
451	  (`SYS_GETINFO` + 13 stubs returning `OK`/`ENOSYS`). A stub task
452	  `SENDREC`s `SYSTEM` for `SYS_GETINFO` and gets a populated reply.
453	
454	Aggregate scope:
455

file.content:
`RTS_NO_PRIV`, contiguous priv-ids 0–15). `kernel-shared` migrated
  `ProcNr` / `PrivId` / `SysId` from `i32` aliases to newtypes,
  added `NR_SYS_CALLS = 32` and a new `sys_limits` module
  (`NR_IO_RANGE`, `NR_IRQ`, `NR_MEM_RANGE`). `ArchRegisterFrame` stub
  in `arch/aarch64/context.rs` ready for slice 2.3 to populate. 25
  host-side tests pass (21 existing + 4 new newtype round-trips);
  clean release build with no warnings.
- **Slice 2.3** ✓ shipped (PR #5, merged 2026-05-22) — aarch64 SVC entry
  cooperative context switch. `entry.S`
  promotes the kernel from Limine's EL1t to EL1h on a primed SP_EL1
  (`mov x9, sp; msr SPSel, #1; mov sp, x9` — the only sequence that
  worked on QEMU virt / Cortex-A72; a bare `msr sp_el1, xN` from EL1t
  silently locks the core). New `arch/aarch64/{mmu,userland,trap,
  user_stub}.S/rs` build a minimal TTBR0_EL1 walk that maps one EL0
  code page (RO+X) and one EL0 stack page (RW+NX) at low VAs, copies a
  hand-coded `svc #0; b .` stub blob into the code page, and erets into
  EL0. Vector slot 8 (`el0_64_sync`) is specialized to `b
  el0_64_sync_entry` in `trap.S`; the SVC handler parks `&mut p.regs`
  in `TPIDR_EL1` so it can save state directly into the proc table and
  tail-call back to EL0 via `el1_return_to_user`. `do_ipc(frame)` in
  the new `kernel/src/ipc/` module prints each call and stores `OK` in
  `frame.x[0]`. `proc::sched::switch_to_user` wraps the eret. Verified
  in QEMU: `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 src_dst=32766 msg=0x0...` repeats
  ~16K times per second.
- **Slice 2.4** ◀ next — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues. Two
  EL0 stub tasks print interleaved bursts proving timer-driven
  preemption.
- **Slice 2.5** — IPC primitives (`mini_send`, `mini_receive`,
  `mini_notify`, `mini_senda`, `mini_sendnb`, `deadlock_check`). Two
  tasks ping-pong messages. **This is the Phase 2 milestone.**
- **Slice 2.6** — Kernel-call dispatch and the minimum `SYS_*` set
  (`SYS_GETINFO` + 13 stubs returning `OK`/`ENOSYS`). A stub task
  `SENDREC`s `SYSTEM` for `SYS_GETINFO` and gets a populated reply.

Aggregate scope:

- `kernel-shared`: Message struct, Endpoint, all call numbers, error codes
- Process table (`Proc`), privilege table (`Priv`)
- IPC: `mini_send`, `mini_receive`, `mini_notify`, `deadlock_check`
- aarch64 arch: exception vectors, GICv3, ARM generic timer, SVC entry, context switch
- Run queues: `enqueue`, `dequeue`, `pick_proc`, `switch_to_user`
- Kernel calls: minimum set (SYS_GETINFO, SYS_PRIVCTL, SYS_FORK, SYS_EXEC, SYS_EXIT, SYS_COPY, SYS_SAFECOPY, SYS_IRQCTL, SYS_VMCTL, SYS_SCHEDULE, SYS_SETALARM, SYS_TIMES, SYS_DIAGCTL, SYS_SETGRANT)
- Boot image unpacking, load two test processes
- **Milestone:** Two processes exchange IPC messages ("ping-pong test")

### Phase 3: VM Server + Memory Management

- `servers/vm/`: Page fault handling, mmap, brk/sbrk
- Kernel: aarch64 translation table creation per process, do_vmctl complete
- fork() clones address space, exec() loads new ELF
- **Milestone:** Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults

### Phase 4: Core Servers (PM, VFS, RS, DS, SCHED)

- `server-rt`: SEF implementation (startup, receive loop, ping/signal handling)

input.new_string:
- **Slice 2.4** ◀ ready (branch `feature/phase-2-4-gicv3-timer-runqueues`,
  pending merge) — GICv3 + ARM generic timer + run queues. New
  `arch/aarch64/{gic,timer,irq}.rs` + `interrupt.S` bring up GICD/GICR
  (QEMU virt cortex-a72, `-machine virt,gic-version=3`), enable PPI 27
  (EL1 virtual timer) at 100 Hz, and route the IRQ through vector slot
  9 → `el0_64_irq_entry` (mirrors slice 2.3's slot-8 treatment) →
  `do_irq` → ICC_IAR1_EL1 ack / `clock::tick` / ICC_EOIR1_EL1. New
  `proc::sched` adds priority-banded FIFO run queues (`enqueue`,
  `dequeue`, `pick_proc`, `reschedule`, `run`) chained through
  `Proc::next_ready: Option<ProcNr>`. New `kernel/src/clock.rs` owns
  the per-tick handler — prints `current_proc.name[0]` to PL011, then
  decrements `quantum_left` and triggers `reschedule` on
  `RTS_NO_QUANTUM`. `userland_bootstrap` now stages two EL0 stubs (A
  and B at `0x40_0000`/`0x41_0000` sharing one code page, distinct
  stacks at `0x80_0000`/`0x81_0000`); SPSR drops to `0x340` so IRQs
  are unmasked at EL0. Verified in QEMU: 171 A ticks vs 170 B ticks
  over ~3.4 s, clean `AAAAA BBBBB AAAAA …` 5-per-quantum bursts with
  `do_ipc[N]` SVC traces interleaved (proves SVC + IRQ paths coexist).
- **Slice 2.5** ◀ next — IPC primitives (`mini_send`, `mini_receive`,
  `mini_notify`, `mini_senda`, `mini_sendnb`, `deadlock_check`). Two
  tasks ping-pong messages. **This is the Phase 2 milestone.**

content.text:
Slice 2.4 is implemented and boot-verified. Summary for your review before commit:

**New files (5):**
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/gic.rs` — GICv3 driver (GICD + GICR + ICC_*_EL1)
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/timer.rs` — CNTV setup at 100 Hz
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/interrupt.S` — `el0_64_irq_entry` (mirrors slot-8 idiom)
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/irq.rs` — `do_irq` (ack → dispatch → eoi)
- `kernel/src/clock.rs` — per-tick handler (prints `name[0]`, decrements quantum, reschedules)

**Modified (9):** vectors.S slot 9 wired; userland.rs grows to two stubs (A/B sharing code page, distinct stacks, SPSR `0x340`); proc::sched gets real run queues (`enqueue`/`dequeue`/`pick_proc`/`reschedule`/`run`) with `Proc::next_ready` linkage; main.rs runs the new boot sequence; build.rs picks up interrupt.S; qemu-run.sh pins `gic-version=3`; docs/plan.md flipped to "ready".

**Verification:**
- `cargo kernel-aarch64` — clean release build, zero warnings
- `cargo check --workspace` — passes
- `cargo test -p minix4-kernel-shared` — 25/25 host tests pass
- QEMU boot over ~3.4s of EL0 runtime: **171 A ticks vs 170 B ticks**, steady-state pattern `AAAAA BBBBB AAAAA BBBBB ...` (5 ticks per quantum), `do_ipc[N]` SVC traces interleave with timer ticks — both SVC and IRQ paths coexist as designed.

I'll stop here for your review of the staged changes — auto mode rule from your CLAUDE.md says not to commit without your sign-off. Let me know when you'd like me to commit (and whether to run `/claude-md-management:revise-claude-md` before the PR step).

content.text:
Review this session for learnings about working with Claude Code in this codebase. Update CLAUDE.md with context that would help future Claude sessions be more effective.

## Step 1: Reflect

What context was missing that would have helped Claude work more effectively?
- Bash commands that were used or discovered
- Code style patterns followed
- Testing approaches that worked
- Environment/configuration quirks
- Warnings or gotchas encountered

## Step 2: Find CLAUDE.md Files

```bash
find . -name "CLAUDE.md" -o -name ".claude.local.md" 2>/dev/null | head -20
```

Decide where each addition belongs:
- `CLAUDE.md` - Team-shared (checked into git)
- `.claude.local.md` - Personal/local only (gitignored)

## Step 3: Draft Additions

**Keep it concise** - one line per concept. CLAUDE.md is part of the prompt, so brevity matters.

Format: `<command or pattern>` - `<brief description>`

Avoid:
- Verbose explanations
- Obvious information
- One-off fixes unlikely to recur

## Step 4: Show Proposed Changes

For each addition:

```
### Update: ./CLAUDE.md

**Why:** [one-line reason]

\`\`\`diff
+ [the addition - keep it brief]
\`\`\`
```

## Step 5: Apply with Approval

Ask if the user wants to apply the changes. Only edit files they approve.

## Step 1: Reflection

What was non-obvious or would have helped a future session:

1. **Boot rationale changed**: CLAUDE.md still says `timeout` is needed because the kernel halts in `wfe`. After slice 2.4, EL0 stubs spin forever — `timeout` is still required, but the reason is different.
2. **`.S` files must be registered**: New assembly files require an entry in `kernel/build.rs`'s `sources` array — easy to forget; not glob-driven.
3. **No cross-`.S` include**: Each `.S` file duplicates needed `.equ` blocks (REGS_*_OFFSET pattern in `trap.S` ↔ `interrupt.S`). This is a real gotcha if you assume includes work.
4. **`drop(&mut T)` is a no-op**: For kernel code with `&mut` followed by an unsafe call that re-borrows the same static, capture state into locals (a bool, a copied scalar) and let NLL end the borrow — don't call `drop(&mut x)`.
5. **Run-queue admission is decoupled from `IMAGE.runnable`**: The boot-image flag governs IPC reachability, not scheduler enqueueing — only `sched::enqueue` admits procs to a run queue.

## Step 2: CLAUDE.md files

Only `./CLAUDE.md` exists. No `.claude.local.md`.

## Step 3 & 4: Proposed changes

### Update: `./CLAUDE.md`

**Why:** Boot rationale changed (kernel no longer halts in wfe after slice 2.4); `timeout` is still needed, but for a different reason.

```diff
- # Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh); `timeout` lets
- # QEMU exit since the kernel halts in `wfe`.
- timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release
+ # Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh). The kernel runs
+ # indefinitely once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+), so `timeout` is mandatory.
+ # Redirect to a file when you need to grep tick output — live tail loses lines.
+ timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release
```

### Update: `./CLAUDE.md` — Code Conventions section

**Why:** Capture three project-specific gotchas a future session is likely to hit when touching kernel internals.

```diff
  ## Code Conventions
  
  - Kernel `unsafe` blocks require `// SAFETY:` comments documenting the invariant
  - IPC linked lists use `Option<ProcNr>` indices into static arrays, not raw pointers
  - Message types are defined in `kernel-shared` and shared across all crates
  - Assembly is confined to `.S` files (assembled via `cc` crate in `build.rs`); use `core::arch::asm!` only for single-instruction operations
+ - New `.S` files must be added to `kernel/build.rs`'s `sources` array; offset blocks (`.equ REGS_*_OFFSET …`) are duplicated per-file since there is no cross-`.S` include
+ - To end a `&mut` borrow before an `unsafe` call that re-borrows the same static, capture state into locals (bool / scalar) and rely on NLL — `drop(&mut x)` is a no-op and triggers a `dropping_references` warning
+ - Run-queue admission is decoupled from boot: `IMAGE.runnable` marks IPC reachability; only `proc::sched::enqueue` puts a proc in the scheduler's run queue
  - Static mutable tables use `UnsafeCell<[T; N]>` inside a `#[repr(transparent)]` newtype with `unsafe impl Sync`; document the single-threaded-boot invariant in the `// SAFETY:` comment
```

## Step 5: Apply?

These are the additions I think are worth keeping — they each cost ~1 line and cover real cliffs a future Claude could fall off. Want me to apply both updates, or trim any of them?

# MINIX 4

MINIX 4 is a learning OS with a Rust microkernel, based on MINIX 3's architecture.

## Project Overview

- **Kernel:** Rust (no_std, no_main), greenfield microkernel
- **Architecture:** aarch64 primary (Apple Silicon / QEMU virt), x86_64 secondary
- **Servers:** PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED as user-space Rust processes
- **Drivers:** VirtIO (MMIO for aarch64, PCI for x86_64) as user-space processes
- **C library:** musl-libc fork with MINIX IPC syscall wrappers
- **License:** BSD-2-Clause only (no GPL code)
- **Platform:** QEMU as primary target

## Reference Codebase

MINIX 4's architecture is based on MINIX 3. When the docs reference "MINIX 3 source",
they mean paths within the MINIX 3 source tree (e.g., `kernel/proc.c` means the
`kernel/proc.c` file in a MINIX 3 checkout). Key reference files:

- `kernel/proc.c` -- IPC implementation (mini_send, mini_receive, deadlock detection)
- `kernel/proc.h`, `priv.h` -- Process and privilege structures
- `kernel/system.c` -- Kernel call dispatch
- `include/minix/ipc.h` -- Message structure definitions
- `include/minix/com.h`, `callnr.h` -- Server endpoints, call numbers
- `lib/libc/sys/*.c` -- POSIX syscall wrappers (template for musl adaptation)
- `lib/libsys/sef.c` -- SEF framework (template for server-rt)

The MINIX 3 source is available at https://github.com/Stichting-MINIX-Research-Foundation/minix

## Build

```sh
# Build kernel for aarch64 (primary target)
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh); `timeout` lets
# QEMU exit since the kernel halts in `wfe`.
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release

# Build kernel for x86_64
cargo kernel-x86_64
```

## Architecture

See `docs/architecture.md` for the full system design. Key concepts:

- **Microkernel:** Only IPC, scheduling, interrupt dispatch, and memory protection in kernel
- **Message passing:** 6 IPC primitives (SEND, RECEIVE, SENDREC, NOTIFY, SENDNB, SENDA)
- **User-space servers:** All OS services (file system, process management, memory management) run as separate processes communicating via IPC
- **Privilege model:** Fine-grained bitmaps control which processes can communicate and what kernel calls they can make

## Workspace Crates

| Crate | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `kernel` | Microkernel (no_std) |
| `kernel-shared` | Message types, endpoints, call numbers shared between kernel and userspace |
| `minix-ipc` | User-space IPC library (SVC/SYSCALL asm stubs) |
| `server-rt` | Server runtime / SEF framework |
| `servers/*` | System servers (PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED) |
| `drivers/*` | Device drivers (VirtIO block/net/console, memory) |
| `fs/*` | File system servers (MFS, PFS) |
| `userland/*` | User programs (init, sh, coreutils) |

## Code Conventions

- Kernel `unsafe` blocks require `// SAFETY:` comments documenting the invariant
- IPC linked lists use `Option<ProcNr>` indices into static arrays, not raw pointers
- Message types are defined in `kernel-shared` and shared across all crates
- Assembly is confined to `.S` files (assembled via `cc` crate in `build.rs`); use `core::arch::asm!` only for single-instruction operations
- Static mutable tables use `UnsafeCell<[T; N]>` inside a `#[repr(transparent)]` newtype with `unsafe impl Sync`; document the single-threaded-boot invariant in the `// SAFETY:` comment
- Custom `Display` impls that must honor `{:<width$}` render through a stack buffer (`arrayvec::ArrayString<N>`) and call `f.pad(s)` — `write!(f, ...)` from inside `Display::fmt` ignores the outer width spec
- Forward declarations intended for later slices (constants, fields, re-exports) get module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` with a one-line comment naming the consuming slice

## Documentation

`docs/plan.md` tracks slice status with three markers: `◀ next` (unstarted), `◀ ready (branch ..., pending merge)` (implemented but unmerged), `✓ shipped (PR #N, merged YYYY-MM-DD)` (merged). Flip the previous slice forward and slide `◀ next` ahead as part of each slice's PR.

- Assembly is confined to `.S` files (assembled via `cc` crate in `build.rs`); use `core::arch::asm!` only for single-instruction operations
+- New `.S` files must be added to `kernel/build.rs`'s `sources` array; offset blocks (`.equ REGS_*_OFFSET …`) are duplicated per-file since there is no cross-`.S` include

+- To end a `&mut` borrow before an `unsafe` call that re-borrows the same static, capture state into locals (bool / scalar) and rely on NLL — `drop(&mut x)` is a no-op and triggers a `dropping_references` warning
+- Run-queue admission is decoupled from boot: `IMAGE.runnable` marks IPC reachability; only `proc::sched::enqueue` puts a proc in the scheduler's run queue
- Static mutable tables use `UnsafeCell<[T; N]>` inside a `#[repr(transparent)]` newtype with `unsafe impl Sync`; document the single-threaded-boot invariant in the `// SAFETY:` comment

- Custom `Display` impls that must honor `{:<width$}` render through a stack buffer (`arrayvec::ArrayString<N>`) and call `f.pad(s)` — `write!(f, ...)` from inside `Display::fmt` ignores the outer width spec
- Forward declarations intended for later slices (constants, fields, re-exports) get module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` with a one-line comment naming the consuming slice

content.text: Both updates applied to `CLAUDE.md`. The file is now staged for the slice 2.4 commit alongside the other changes. Let me know when you'd like me to commit (and whether to push + open the PR after).

content.content:
phase 2.3: aarch64 SVC entry + cooperative EL0 switch

First context that runs at EL0 in MINIX 4. `entry.S` promotes the kernel
from Limine's EL1t to EL1h on a primed SP_EL1 (`mov x9, sp; msr SPSel,
#1; mov sp, x9` — bare `msr sp_el1, xN` from EL1t silently locks
Cortex-A72 on QEMU virt). New `arch/aarch64/{mmu,userland,trap,
user_stub}` build a minimal TTBR0_EL1 walk that maps one EL0 code page
(RO+X) and one EL0 stack page (RW+NX), copies a `mov…; svc #0; b .`
stub blob into the code page, and erets into EL0. Vector slot 8 is
specialized to `el0_64_sync_entry` in `trap.S`; the SVC handler parks
`&mut Proc.regs` in TPIDR_EL1 so it saves state directly into the proc
table and tail-calls back to EL0 via `el1_return_to_user`. New
`kernel/src/ipc/` ships `do_ipc` as a stub that logs each call;
`proc::sched::switch_to_user` wraps the eret. Verified in QEMU: ~16K
do_ipc round-trips per second.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: b627578f8a93

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
phase 2.4: GICv3 + ARM virtual timer + run queues

First preemptive context switch in MINIX 4. Two EL0 stub tasks share a
single TTBR0 walk (code at 0x40_0000/0x41_0000, distinct stacks at
0x80_0000/0x81_0000); each ticks for 5 quanta of 10 ms before the
scheduler swaps them, producing the expected `AAAAA BBBBB AAAAA …`
bursts in the boot log.

New `arch/aarch64/{gic,timer,irq}.rs` + `interrupt.S` bring up GICD
(`0x0800_0000`) and CPU 0's GICR (`0x080A_0000`) on QEMU virt cortex-a72
with `-machine virt,gic-version=3`, enable PPI 27 (EL1 virtual timer)
at priority 0x80, and program CNTV_TVAL/CTL for 100 Hz. Vector slot 9
is specialized to `el0_64_irq_entry` (mirrors slice 2.3's slot-8
treatment) which saves the EL0 frame into TPIDR_EL1-pointed regs and
calls `do_irq` → ICC_IAR1_EL1 ack / `clock::tick` / ICC_EOIR1_EL1.

`proc::sched` grows real priority-banded FIFO run queues
(`enqueue`/`dequeue`/`pick_proc`/`reschedule`/`run`) chained through a
new `Proc::next_ready: Option<ProcNr>` field — `RUNQ` is the same
`UnsafeCell` + `Sync` newtype pattern as PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE since
the kernel runs DAIF-masked and the IRQ stub is the only async writer.
`kernel/src/clock.rs` owns the per-tick handler: prints
`current_proc.name[0]` to PL011, decrements `quantum_left`, and on
exhaustion sets `RTS_NO_QUANTUM` + calls `sched::reschedule`, which
re-enqueues current at tail and rewrites TPIDR_EL1 so the IRQ stub's
trailing `el1_return_to_user` erets into the new context.

Stub SPSR drops from 0x3C0 to 0x340 (clears bit 7) so EL0 runs with
IRQs unmasked; the kernel stays DAIF-masked. EL1 IRQs (vector slot 5)
remain a hard panic.

Verified in QEMU over ~3.4 s of EL0 runtime: 171 A ticks vs 170 B
ticks, steady-state 5-per-quantum bursts, `do_ipc[N]` SVC traces
interleave with timer ticks (proves SVC + IRQ paths coexist). Clean
release build, host workspace check passes, 25/25 kernel-shared host
tests pass.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log -1 --stat | head -25
